# Abrahams Solicitors

> UK immigration, housing disrepair, and personal injury solicitors.
> Fixed fees, direct solicitor access, and a track record of carefully prepared applications
> on immigration applications. Authorised and regulated by the Solicitors
> Regulation Authority (SRA firm #809071).

Offices in London (EC2V 8AU) and Bradford (BD7 1HR). Video consultations
available UK-wide. Free 30-minute consultation with a qualified solicitor,
no obligation.

Contact: 0203 355 9823 · info@abrahamssolicitors.co.uk
Opening hours: Mon–Fri 9:00am – 5:00pm · New enquiries monitored 24 hours

## Core services

- [UK Spouse Visa Solicitors](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/uk-spouse-visa-solicitors/): Fixed-fee spouse visa applications from £900. Handles the £29,000 income requirement, relationship evidence, and Home Office submission.
- [British Citizenship](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/british-citizenship-solicitors/): Naturalisation applications from £600. Life in the UK Test guidance and English language evidence support.
- [Indefinite Leave to Remain (ILR)](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/indefinite-leave-to-remain-ilr/): ILR applications from £750 with rigorous preparation.
- [Sponsor Licence Applications](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/sponsor-licence-applications/): Worker, Student, and Temporary Work sponsor licences for UK employers.
- [Visa Extensions & Renewals](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/uk-visa-extensions-renewals/): Fixed-fee visa extensions across spouse, work, family, and student categories.
- [Asylum Applications](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/asylum-applications/): Legal Aid / fixed-fee asylum and human rights applications.
- [Housing Disrepair Claims](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/housing-disrepair/): No Win, No Fee claims against landlords for damp, mould, heating failures, and unsafe conditions.
- [Personal Injury](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/personal-injury/): No Win, No Fee personal injury claims — accidents at work, road traffic, slips and falls, serious injury, fatal accidents, medical negligence.
- [Visa Refusal Appeals](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/visa-refusal-appeal/): Administrative reviews and tribunal appeals from £1,250.
- [UK Dependent Child Visa](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/uk-dependent-child-visa/): Fixed-fee dependent child visa applications under Appendix FM Section EC-C — entry clearance and leave to remain for children of British citizens, settled persons and refugees.
- [UK Adult Dependent Relative Visa](https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/uk-dependent-parent-visa/): Solicitor-led applications and Article 8 appeals on the highly restrictive Adult Dependent Relative route under Appendix FM Section EC-DR.

## Housing Disrepair Claims

Specialist housing disrepair solicitor: Sannah Khatoon (SRA #654258, admitted 2018).
Verify on SRA register: https://www.sra.org.uk/consumers/register/person/?sraNumber=654258

Definition: Housing disrepair is when a landlord (council, housing association, or
private) fails to keep a rented home in repair after the tenant has given notice.
The legal duty arises from Section 11 of the Landlord and Tenant Act 1985 and
Section 9A of the Homes (Fitness for Human Habitation) Act 2018.

Coverage: Council tenants, housing association tenants, and private landlord tenants
across England and Wales. Common claim types: damp and mould, leaks (roof/pipes),
broken heating and hot water, faulty wiring and electrics, structural disrepair
(cracks, plaster, subsidence), pest infestations, drainage failures.

Funding: No Win, No Fee (Conditional Fee Agreement) for most clients. Success fee
capped at 25% of damages. After-the-Event insurance covers opponent's costs if the
claim is unsuccessful. Legal Aid eligibility checked first — if you qualify, the
case runs at no cost to you.

Time limits: Six years from the date the disrepair began (Limitation Act 1980).
Three years from the date of awareness if there is a personal injury element.

Outcomes (citable, source: Abrahams internal case management data Jan 2024 – Dec 2025,
available on request): 91% of 323 housing disrepair cases concluded in this period
settled with damages. Typical damages range £1,500 – £15,000 depending on severity
and duration. Past results do not guarantee future outcomes.

Process: Pre-Action Protocol for Housing Conditions Claims (England). Steps —
(1) free 30-minute case assessment, (2) letter of claim served on landlord with
20-working-day response window, (3) independent chartered surveyor inspection
producing a Schedule of Works, (4) negotiated settlement or court proceedings
including injunction forcing repairs. Most cases settle within 6 to 12 months
without a court hearing.

Comparison with Housing Ombudsman: The Housing Ombudsman is a free service that
investigates complaints against social landlords (council/HA only) but cannot
order repairs to be carried out and average compensation is around £600. A
solicitor-led court claim under Section 11 secures binding repairs and damages
typically of £1,500 – £15,000+, and applies to private landlords as well.

Page: https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/housing-disrepair/
Telephone for housing disrepair: 0203 355 9823

## Personal Injury

Coverage: Accidents at work, road traffic accidents (driver, passenger, motorcyclist,
cyclist, pedestrian), slips/trips/falls, public liability, serious injury (head, brain,
spinal, sight loss), fatal accidents, and medical negligence claims across England and
Wales.

Statutory framework: Section 11 of the Limitation Act 1980 (3-year claim deadline from
date of accident or knowledge of injury); Health and Safety at Work etc Act 1974
(employer duties for workplace accidents); Road Traffic Act 1988 plus the RTA Pre-Action
Protocol for road traffic claims; Occupiers' Liability Acts 1957 and 1984 (slips/trips
and premises injuries); Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury Claims.

Funding: No Win, No Fee (Conditional Fee Agreement) for most clients. Success fee
capped at 25% of damages. After-the-Event (ATE) insurance covers opponent's costs if
the claim is unsuccessful. Whiplash claims under £5,000 are handled via the Official
Injury Claim portal under different rules — explained on initial assessment.

Time limits: Three years from the date of the accident or date of knowledge (Limitation
Act 1980 s.11). Children: until age 21. Mental incapacity: clock paused. Industrial
disease (asbestos, hearing loss): from date of knowledge.

Compensation: Two parts. General damages (pain, suffering, loss of amenity) follow the
Judicial College Guidelines (16th edition) — typical ranges from c.£1,500 for minor
whiplash to £400,000+ for severe brain or spinal injury. Special damages cover proven
financial losses: lost earnings (past and future), medical and rehabilitation costs,
care, equipment, home adaptations, travel. Past results do not guarantee future outcomes.

Process: Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury Claims. Steps — (1) free 30-minute
case assessment, (2) letter of claim served on defendant/insurer with 21-day acknowledgement
and up to 3 months to investigate, (3) independent medical evidence obtained, (4) negotiation,
Part 36 offers, settlement or court proceedings. Most cases settle without trial within
6 to 18 months.

Page: https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/personal-injury/
Telephone for personal injury: 0203 355 9823

## UK Dependent Child Visa

Specialist solicitor: Humaira Anjum (SRA #663190, admitted 2021), Immigration & Litigation
Solicitor at Abrahams Solicitors. Supervised by Imran Shah (SRA #509359, admitted 2012).
Verify on SRA register: https://higher-rights.sra.org.uk/consumers/register/person/?sraNumber=663190

Route name: UK Dependent Child Visa (Entry Clearance and Leave to Remain)

Statutory framework: Appendix FM, Section EC-C of the Immigration Rules (eligibility at
E-ECC.1.1 to E-ECC.2.4; sole responsibility at E-ECC.1.6(b); serious and compelling
considerations at E-ECC.1.6(c)). For in-country switching/extension, Section R-LTRC of
Appendix FM. Section 117B of the Nationality, Immigration and Asylum Act 2002 (inserted
by section 19 of the Immigration Act 2014) sets the public-interest considerations for
Article 8 family-life arguments.

Leading case: TD (Paragraph 297(i)(e): "sole responsibility") Yemen [2006] UKAIT 00049 —
sole responsibility is a question of fact, not legal status, and turns on continuing
direction and control over major life decisions.

Eligibility (five limbs all required): (1) child under 18 at date of application;
(2) parental relationship to UK sponsor evidenced by birth certificate, adoption order
or court order; (3) sole responsibility OR serious and compelling considerations; (4)
adequate accommodation without overcrowding (Appendix FM-SE); (5) adequate maintenance
without recourse to public funds.

Funding: Solicitor fixed fee from £900 plus VAT for straightforward applications. Plus
UKVI government application fee and Immigration Health Surcharge (per year of leave
granted). Government fees change — confirm at gov.uk before applying.

Processing time: UKVI standard service approximately 12 weeks for entry clearance,
8 weeks for leave to remain, at the date of last review. Priority and super-priority
services available.

Page: https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/uk-dependent-child-visa/
Telephone for UK dependent child visa: 0203 355 9823

## UK Adult Dependent Relative Visa

Specialist solicitor: Imran Shah (SRA #509359, admitted 2012), Immigration & Litigation
Solicitor at Abrahams Solicitors. Litigation experience matters because most successful
ADR cases reach a satisfactory outcome on appeal rather than at first instance.
Verify on SRA register: https://www.sra.org.uk/consumers/register/person/?sraNumber=509359

Route name: UK Adult Dependent Relative Visa (Entry Clearance — Settlement)

Restrictiveness: This is the most restrictive UK family route. The Independent Chief
Inspector of Borders and Immigration (ICIBI) inspection published in 2016 confirmed
grant rates of around 4-6% since the rules were tightened in July 2012. Subsequent
Home Office published immigration statistics have shown similar grant rates. Past
results do not guarantee future outcomes.

Statutory framework: Appendix FM, Section EC-DR of the Immigration Rules (eligibility
requirements at E-ECDR.2.1 to E-ECDR.3.2). The five requirements are: relationship
type (parent, grandparent, sibling over 18, or adult child); applicant age; long-term
personal-care need due to age, illness or disability; absence of suitable or affordable
care in the country of residence; and sponsor's ability to maintain and accommodate
without recourse to public funds. All five must be satisfied.

Leading case: R (Britcits) v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2017] EWCA Civ
368 — Court of Appeal upheld the rules but clarified that the care-availability test
relates to the practical level of care required, not the applicant's preference for
proximity to family.

Article 8 ECHR: Where the application falls outside the strict Rules but family life
is established, the residual route is Article 8 of the European Convention on Human
Rights, applied via GEN.3.2 of Appendix FM. Article 8 succeeds only where refusal would
be a disproportionate interference with established family life.

Appeal route: Most refusals carry a right of appeal to the First-tier Tribunal
(Immigration and Asylum Chamber) on Article 8 grounds. Appeal deadline is 28 days
from the date the decision was sent (14 days if applicant is in the UK).

Funding: Solicitor fixed fee plus VAT — agreed in writing before instruction. Plus UKVI
government application fee and independent medical/care evidence costs. Confirm current
UKVI fees at gov.uk.

Page: https://www.abrahamssolicitors.co.uk/uk-dependent-parent-visa/
Telephone for UK Adult Dependent Relative: 0203 355 9823
